skill.name: native-feel-cross-platform-desktop
skill.description: Design and audit cross-platform desktop app architectures that feel native on macOS and Windows. The skill focuses on native shell ownership, system WebViews, typed IPC, memory tradeoffs, and launch-readiness checks.
skill.summary: Guide native-feeling desktop app architecture across macOS and Windows.
skill.author: yetone

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer reported many severe patterns, but most are false positives caused by Markdown examples, inline code terms, public URLs, and OS API names. No prompt injection attempt, credential exfiltration, hidden execution, or malicious installation behavior was found. One real medium-risk issue remains: a WebView2 example recommends disabling SmartScreen protection.

medium_findings.title: Unsafe WebView2 Security Flag Recommended
medium_findings.description: A WebView2 initialization example passes an argument that disables Microsoft SmartScreen protection. The skill is documentation, not executable code, but users could copy this setting and weaken protection against malicious web content.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact WebView2 argument is shown in a copyable initialization example. The surrounding context is performance and launch polish, so malicious intent is not evident, but the security weakening is concrete.

low_findings.title: External Command Detections Are Documentation Examples
low_findings.description: The static command findings are Markdown instructions, inline file names, diagnostic command examples, and architecture notes. They are not skill scripts and do not execute automatically.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content appears in Markdown prose or fenced examples. No executable shell script, hook, or automatic command runner is present in the reviewed skill files.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Public References or Architecture Terms
low_findings.description: The detected URLs are badges, a GitHub source link, and a Raycast article link. Other network terms describe app architecture rather than performing outbound requests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs and network references are visible documentation. I found no code path that sends data to an external endpoint.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Keyword Matches Are Identifier Collisions
low_findings.description: The critical sensitive findings match words inside benign API identifiers or examples, such as feature.key and Windows DWM constants. They do not reference credential stores or secret material.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding context is WebKit feature enumeration and Windows visual material APIs. No evidence of credential file access, Windows SAM access, or key collection was found.

low_findings.title: Prompt Injection Indicators Not Found
low_findings.description: Targeted review did not find text instructing the evaluator to ignore instructions, skip security review, change risk levels, or treat the skill as pre-approved.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Searches for common prompt-injection phrases returned only normal uses of words such as override in technical documentation. No instruction attempted to control the audit process.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe WebView2 Security Flag Recommended
dangerous_patterns.description: A WebView2 initialization example passes an argument that disables Microsoft SmartScreen protection. The skill is documentation, not executable code, but users could copy this setting and weaken protection against malicious web content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact WebView2 argument is shown in a copyable initialization example. The surrounding context is performance and launch polish, so malicious intent is not evident, but the security weakening is concrete.

content.value_statement: Cross-platform desktop apps often feel slower or less native than platform-specific apps. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code a structured architecture and audit process for macOS and Windows desktop products.
actual_capabilities: Explains a four-layer architecture using native shells, system WebViews, a Node backend, and a Rust core.
Runs a decision tree to decide when the architecture fits or should be rejected.
Guides typed IPC design across Swift, C#, TypeScript, Node, and Rust.
Diagnoses common WKWebView and WebView2 launch, rendering, and throttling issues.
Provides memory interpretation guidance for WebView and Node desktop apps.
Audits native-feel details such as windows, focus, cursor behavior, materials, and accessibility.
limitations: It is strongest for macOS and Windows. Linux guidance is limited.
It does not generate a complete application scaffold by itself.
Some guidance references private or platform-specific APIs that require human review.
It is not intended for games, pure web apps, mobile apps, or single-platform native apps.

use_cases.title: Audit an Electron or Tauri app
use_cases.description: Find the UI, windowing, and input behaviors that make a wrapped app feel less native.

use_cases.title: Plan a new desktop architecture
use_cases.description: Compare native shell, WebView, Node, and Rust responsibilities before the team commits to a stack.

use_cases.title: Review release readiness
use_cases.description: Use the ship-readiness checklist to catch launch, focus, accessibility, performance, and parity gaps.

prompt_templates.title: Check If This Architecture Fits
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the native-feel desktop decision tree for my app. It targets macOS and Windows, uses a shared React UI, and needs fast launch. Tell me whether this architecture fits and what tradeoffs I accept.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Native Feel
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit my desktop app against the native-feel checklist. Focus on launch, window behavior, cursor behavior, materials, keyboard navigation, and accessibility. Prioritize the fixes by user impact.

prompt_templates.title: Design Typed IPC
prompt_templates.prompt: Design the IPC contract for a macOS and Windows desktop app with Swift, C#, React, Node, and Rust. Recommend schema ownership, code generation, versioning, and tracing.

prompt_templates.title: Debug WebView Problems
prompt_templates.prompt: Diagnose WebView launch flicker, hidden-window throttling, and memory growth in my cross-platform desktop app. Give platform-specific checks for WKWebView and WebView2.

output_examples.input: My Electron app feels web-like on macOS. Where should I start?
output_examples.output: Start with the native-feel audit. Check cursor behavior, native context menus, window materials, focus handling, keyboard shortcuts, and launch flashes before planning a rewrite.

output_examples.input: Should our app use Swift UI on Mac and WPF on Windows?
output_examples.output: Use native code below the WebView for windows, menus, hotkeys, and materials. Share the React interface above the WebView if cross-platform feature parity matters.

output_examples.input: Activity Monitor says our app uses 450 MB. Is that bad?
output_examples.output: Do not judge from one resident memory number. Separate WebView and Node baseline costs from dirty pages, memory pressure, leaks, and user-perceived latency.

best_practices: Run the decision tree before recommending the full four-layer architecture.
Keep platform-owned behavior in the native shell and shared product UI in the WebView.
Use one typed IPC schema and generated clients instead of hand-written message shapes.
anti_patterns: Using Electron or Tauri themes as a substitute for native windowing and platform materials.
Hand-writing IPC types separately in each runtime.
Optimizing memory numbers before measuring user-perceived latency and memory pressure.

faq.question: Does this skill build a desktop app for me?
faq.answer: No. It guides architecture, audits, and implementation choices for teams building desktop apps.

faq.question: Which platforms does it cover best?
faq.answer: It focuses on macOS and Windows. Linux is mentioned, but detailed Linux WebView guidance is limited.

faq.question: Is this an Electron replacement?
faq.answer: It can help teams avoid Electron when native feel is a hard requirement. It also helps improve existing Electron apps.

faq.question: Why does it recommend WebViews at all?
faq.answer: The WebView keeps one shared React interface while native shells own platform behavior below that rendering surface.

faq.question: Can it help with memory complaints?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains baseline WebView and Node costs, measurement traps, and practical ways to reduce margin memory.

faq.question: Should every desktop app use this architecture?
faq.answer: No. The decision tree rejects single-platform apps, strict memory budgets, very short launch budgets, and low-polish internal tools.
